is_p_falciparum.evidence: The paper explicitly studies P. falciparum infection in relation to sickle cell genotypes in Western Kenya. Title and methods confirm P. falciparum detection via blood film microscopy.

reports_target_markers.evidence: The study reports presence/absence of P. falciparum parasites (malaria positive/negative) by microscopy, but does NOT report frequencies of specific drug-resistance codon positions. No mention of Pfcrt, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfmdr1, or Pfcytb marker variants.

reports_extractable_frequencies.evidence: The paper provides overall malaria prevalence percentages by genotype (44.1% in HbAA, 36.2% in HbAS, 20% in HbSS) but does not report numerator/denominator counts or percentages for any specific drug-resistance loci. No codon-level frequency data is presented.

sub_country_location.evidence: The study is restricted to 'West Kenyan residents of the Kisumu area' near Lake Victoria. Kisumu is a named sub-national city/region in Kenya, meeting the sub-country location requirement.

collection_window_within_3y.evidence: Blood samples were collected 'over a period of 12 months' (Results section), well within the 3-year window.

african_field_samples.evidence: The study involved indigenous West Kenyan field/clinical populations attending a blood testing laboratory in Kisumu, Kenya, an African country. Samples are described as field isolates from residents of a malaria-endemic area.

is_primary_study.evidence: This is a primary empirical cross-sectional survey with original data collection, blood testing, microscopy, and statistical analysis. It is not a review, meta-analysis, or modelling paper.

duplicate_risk.evidence: The paper describes a de novo cross-sectional hospital-based survey of 773 subjects in the Kisumu area over 12 months in 1997. No mention of consortium membership, named cohort, surveillance network, or prior data overlap. Routine field collection with no evidence of duplication with other published studies.

supplement.evidence: No supplementary materials are referenced in the paper. All reported data (malaria prevalence by genotype, relative risks, odds ratios) are presented in the main text and tables. However, because the paper does not report drug-resistance marker frequencies at all, supplementary material cannot rescue this eligibility failure.

assessment.summary: This paper is INELIGIBLE. Although it is a primary study of P. falciparum infection in African field samples (Western Kenya) with appropriate sub-country geographic detail and sample collection window, it FAILS two critical eligibility criteria: (1) it does NOT report frequencies of any curated drug-resistance codon markers (Pfcrt, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfmdr1, Pfcytb), and (2) it does NOT provide extractable numerator/denominator or per-locus frequency data. The study focuses exclusively on overall malaria prevalence in relation to sickle cell genotypes and contains no molecular characterization of resistance markers.
exclusion_reasons: Does not report frequencies of any target drug-resistance markers (Pfcrt, Pfdhfr, Pfdhps, Pfk13, Pfmdr1, Pfcytb)
Does not provide extractable numerator/denominator counts or percentages for specific drug-resistance loci; only reports overall malaria presence/absence
